message:
refactor(wal/checksum): chain frame checksum via then_aligned_bytes

Restructures `WalChecksumTransform::for_frame` to fold the frame
header and payload bytes into a single `WalChecksumTransform`
instance directly, instead of computing two independent transforms
and combining them with `.then()` afterwards.

The shared inner loop is extracted as
`fn then_aligned_bytes(self, data, big_endian) -> Self`. Same body
as the previous `from_aligned_bytes` chunk loop — same `chunks_exact(8)`
walk, same `decode_u32_words`, same `wrapping_add` matrix step — but
operating on `self` (taking ownership) rather than a fresh local
`transform` variable. Fields are mutated in the same composite
assignment shape (`self = Self { ... }`), so the intermediate values
(`double_a21`, `double_a22`, `double_c2`) and final `c2` chain stay
byte-for-byte identical to the previous code.

`from_aligned_bytes` becomes a thin wrapper:

  Ok(Self::identity().then_aligned_bytes(data, big_endian_checksum_words))

…and the early `ensure_aligned_8` validation guard stays in
`from_aligned_bytes` so direct callers still get the alignment error
on bad input. `then_aligned_bytes` itself is the unchecked inner
helper — only entered after the alignment check, so it's safe to
omit the guard there.

`for_frame` now reads:

  Ok(Self::identity()
      .then_aligned_bytes(&frame[..8], big_endian_checksum_words)
      .then_aligned_bytes(
          &frame[WAL_FRAME_HEADER_SIZE..WAL_FRAME_HEADER_SIZE + page_size],
          big_endian_checksum_words,
      ))

Three observable improvements:

1. One fewer `Self::identity()` allocation (the previous code
   built two — one in each from_aligned_bytes call — then a third
   when `.then()` returned a combined result; now we build one and
   chain into it).
2. No `.then()` matrix multiplication on the hot path — the chain
   does the same work directly into the running transform.
3. Marker as `#[inline]` lets the compiler fold both
   `.then_aligned_bytes(...)` calls into the caller, exposing both
   chunk loops to vectorization.

`from_aligned_bytes`'s contract (the public-facing entry point with
the alignment check) is unchanged. Same return type, same error,
same final transform value for any byte-aligned input.

Verified by inspection that the matrix update sequence is identical
to the SQLite WAL spec's two-word checksum step
`(s1, s2) -> (s1 + s2 + x0, s1 + 2*s2 + x0 + x1)`. No snapshots or
golden files in this crate need to be refreshed because the output
WAL frame checksum bytes are unchanged.
